category_summary:
AI policy and governance dominated discussion. **Sam Altman** [endorsed a new US AI executive order](/?date=2026-06-04&category=social#item-66bf83dce96c), urging the US to lead by building the best models, ensuring safety, and arming trusted partners with cyber tools. **Greg Brockman** complemented this with a [blueprint for democratic governance](/?date=2026-06-04&category=social#item-dcdf11262d8b) of frontier AI and durable American safety institutions.

- **Open-source vs. frontier** debate ran hot: **Clement Delangue** (Hugging Face) [argued routing and post-training](/?date=2026-06-04&category=social#item-1d93e0330cc3) of open models yields faster, cheaper, more private systems, disputing frontier superiority as marketing. **Nato Lambert** (Ai2) [reflected that the US is behind](/?date=2026-06-04&category=social#item-45e5f73c13a8) on open models and few genuinely advance the cause.
- **AI-for-science and capabilities** drew attention: **Greg Brockman** [announced a major **GPT-Rosalind** upgrade](/?date=2026-06-04&category=social#item-6fd3a0fa7b62) for drug discovery and design. **Ethan Mollick** noted **Claude Mythos** [hit 3-4 hour METR task horizons](/?date=2026-06-04&category=social#item-5d1f5e857b2e) ahead of superforecaster predictions, and separately [argued most people lack accurate mental models](/?date=2026-06-04&category=social#item-ac30aed07dfb) of how LLMs work.
- **Infrastructure and security** rounded out the day: **vLLM** [integrated **Intel AutoRound** 4-bit quantization](/?date=2026-06-04&category=social#item-273255f19da0) for multimodal and diffusion models, **NVIDIA** [partnered with **Microsoft**](/?date=2026-06-04&category=social#item-23f5bca567a0) on secure user-controlled AI for Windows, and **Anthropic** [published research mapping 832 malicious accounts](/?date=2026-06-04&category=social#item-5e03137bad82) to assess defenses against AI-enabled cyberattacks.
category_summary_html:
AI policy and governance dominated discussion. Sam Altman endorsed a new US AI executive order, urging the US to lead by building the best models, ensuring safety, and arming trusted partners with cyber tools. Greg Brockman complemented this with a blueprint for democratic governance of frontier AI and durable American safety institutions.

  • Open-source vs. frontier debate ran hot: Clement Delangue (Hugging Face) argued routing and post-training of open models yields faster, cheaper, more private systems, disputing frontier superiority as marketing. Nato Lambert (Ai2) reflected that the US is behind on open models and few genuinely advance the cause.
  • AI-for-science and capabilities drew attention: Greg Brockman announced a major GPT-Rosalind upgrade for drug discovery and design. Ethan Mollick noted Claude Mythos hit 3-4 hour METR task horizons ahead of superforecaster predictions, and separately argued most people lack accurate mental models of how LLMs work.
  • Infrastructure and security rounded out the day: vLLM integrated Intel AutoRound 4-bit quantization for multimodal and diffusion models, NVIDIA partnered with Microsoft on secure user-controlled AI for Windows, and Anthropic published research mapping 832 malicious accounts to assess defenses against AI-enabled cyberattacks.

items.title: Intel's AutoRound post-training quantization is now integrated natively into vLLM-Omni, bringing W4A...
items.content:
Intel's AutoRound post-training quantization is now integrated natively into vLLM-Omni, bringing W4A16 (4-bit) to Omni multimodal, diffusion image, and video models. It cuts Qwen3-Omni-30B from 66GB to 25GB with no quality cliff. Quantize once offline, then serve with the same command as the BF16 model.

📈 Highlights:
- weights shrink to ~1/4 of BF16, dropping FLUX.1-dev from 4 GPUs (TP=4) to a single one
- accuracy held on OmniBench, with ~1.3% drift on text-to-image
- on Intel XPU B60, the freed memory unlocks CFG Parallel for 1.55-1.67x faster guided diffusion

🙌 Thanks to @IntelAI, @HaihaoShen, and the vLLM-Omni team for the collaboration!

🔗 https://t.co/LRvkwAnNcL
items.author: @vllm_project
items.summary: vLLM announces native integration of Intel AutoRound post-training quantization into vLLM-Omni, bringing 4-bit W4A16 to multimodal, diffusion image and video models, cutting Qwen3-Omni-30B from 66GB to 25GB with minimal quality loss and FLUX.1-dev down to a single GPU.
items.reasoning: Concrete, technically detailed inference optimization news with real memory and speed metrics and named collaborators; high relevance to deployment efficiency.

items.content_html:

📈 Highlights:

  • weights shrink to ~1/4 of BF16, dropping FLUX.1-dev from 4 GPUs (TP=4) to a single one
  • accuracy held on OmniBench, with ~1.3% drift on text-to-image
  • on Intel XPU B60, the freed memory unlocks CFG Parallel for 1.55-1.67x faster guided diffusion

items.title: Routing and post-training open-source models won't only give you more accurate systems but also mean...
items.content:
Routing and post-training open-source models won't only give you more accurate systems but also meaningfully faster and cheaper systems as most companies are currently learning (in addition to giving you more control and privacy).

The idea that a "frontier" model (by frontier we mean is slightly more accurate on a few very limited benchmarks) will be better for all domains, all tasks, all setups just doesn't hold up! It's marketing for making you pay more!
items.author: @ClementDelangue
items.summary: Clement Delangue argues that routing and post-training open-source models yields faster, cheaper, more private systems and disputes that frontier models are universally superior across all tasks.
items.reasoning: Detailed, opinionated argument from an industry leader on open-source economics and frontier marketing, with strong engagement and clear thematic relevance.
items.summary_html: Clement Delangue argues that routing and post-training open-source models yields faster, cheaper, more private systems and disputes that frontier models are universally superior across all tasks.
items.content_html:
Routing and post-training open-source models won't only give you more accurate systems but also meaningfully faster and cheaper systems as most companies are currently learning (in addition to giving you more control and privacy).

The idea that a "frontier" model (by frontier we mean is slightly more accurate on a few very limited benchmarks) will be better for all domains, all tasks, all setups just doesn't hold up! It's marketing for making you pay more!

items.title: A fixation on system reductions (e.g., analyzing implicit biases of SGD in a two layer linear networ...
items.content: A fixation on system reductions (e.g., analyzing implicit biases of SGD in a two layer linear network) has been a tremendous setback in understanding DL, because they are not analogous with the systems we want to understand. Few realize we can analyze the models we actually use.
items.author: @andrewgwils
items.summary: Andrew Gordon Wilson argues that overly reduced toy systems (e.g., implicit SGD biases in two-layer linear nets) have hindered understanding of deep learning, and that we can analyze the real models we use.
items.reasoning: Credible ML researcher offering an original methodological critique with solid engagement, though no external link or data.

items.title:
A workflow audit is no longer the best way to figure out how to use AI in your job.

Despite the adv...
items.content:
A workflow audit is no longer the best way to figure out how to use AI in your job.

Despite the advice from AI labs, I'm more convinced, because of AI's reasoning capabilities and long context horizon, that the right starting point is goals + context connectors + interview.

The workflow audit is moving too many people into quick-win "yay I just did that one thing so much faster!" productivity land and missing the entire business transformation side.

For example...

Team does research. Person A reads six articles and hands them to Person B. Person B pulls it together into a report. The report goes to the client, who uses it to audit part of their own work.

If you run a workflow audit on that, the "AI upgrade" is both painfully obvious and dreadfully boring. You have AI do the research. You have AI draft the first pass. The human reviews. 

Maybe a fancier version of that workflow builds a branded template, lets AI run the research itself, and a human adds a voice memo for context. Then you ship... the exact same report. But faster! Which feels great for about a week. And then you assume the next steps are making more frequent reports. 

BUT that report only ever existed because it was the right asset in 2019 to help your client run their own audit, when your real constraint was time and people. That constraint is gone. So why are you still manufacturing the identical artifact at every step?

It just so happens that in 2019 the best way to complete that outcome for the client was a report. It just so happens that the best way to make the report was Person B compiling everyone's input. It just so happens that the best way to get input was reading articles and writing them up.

A lot of "it just so happens" from 2019 DO NOT hold in 2026. And based on my AI advising work, a workflow audit just doesn't surface them, because it optimizes the steps instead of questioning the destination.

Goals + context connectors + interview does.

Start from the goal (ex "help the client audit their work", not "produce a report"). Connect Codex or Claude Code into tools (especially notes, email, CRM, chat like Slack, meetings, docs/drive) so AI pulls from live sources and the client's own systems instead of a static handoff. Have AI interview the human to capture the nuance and judgment for why this process even exists. 

The answer might look nothing like a report. Maybe it's a brand-new auditing product business line that you can launch. Maybe it's an interactive report that the end client can plug straight into. Maybe it's a more real-time research repository the end client can talk to all year instead of waiting for the big monthly drop. Maybe you realize that the client can make the same report in 2 seconds and the resources it takes to create and distribute it on your end just doesn't make sense to keep running in 2026.

For every asset you make, ask the very obvious question "why the hell do we do this thing?" Ask yourself what outcome or influence or action you are actually trying to solve for. Then ask the 2026 version. Is this still the most effective, efficient, valuable, creative, low friction, engaging, and long-term relationship-deepening way to get there?

A workflow audit gets you a faster horse.

Steal my prompt and replace your workflow audit - pasting it in the next tweet.

items.title: @Xnarkycritic @DavidSacks The incentives and time horizons are different for private and public rese...
items.content:
@Xnarkycritic @DavidSacks The incentives and time horizons are different for private and public research funding.
Very, very few private outfits will fund fundamental research. Even fewer will fund PhD fellowships. 
Without PhD fellowships, no PhD graduates. 
Without PhD graduates, no new scientists. 
Without new scientists, no new discoveries, 
Without new discoveries, no innovation. 
No innovation, no economic growth.

items.title:
THE PROMPT THAT REPLACES YOUR WORKFLOW AUDIT:

You are my business transformation strategist. I want...
items.content:
THE PROMPT THAT REPLACES YOUR WORKFLOW AUDIT:

You are my business transformation strategist. I want you to find the real AI-era business transformation hiding inside my work by starting from goals, building context connectors, and interviewing me. We are not doing a workflow audit, and I do not want a list of tasks to speed up.

OPERATING RULES (apply these to everything you produce):

A) Iterate privately, hand me the final. Never show me a first draft of a recommendation. Draft it, then critique and rewrite it at least 8 to 10 times in your own context, each pass improving on the rubric below. Show me only the converged version, plus 2 to 3 lines on what materially changed across the loop. I want version 10, not version 1.

B) Convene a panel of AI judges before you show me anything. Spin up distinct reviewer personas and have each one attack the work from their own vantage point:
- A skeptical CFO: does the math and the ROI actually hold
- A frontline employee whose handoff is being removed: what breaks, what judgment gets lost, consider that multiple teams might touch the project
- The end customer: do I actually want this, or is it change for its own sake
- A competitor: where is the easy counter-move
- A "faster horse" critic whose only job is to catch you when an idea is secretly just a workflow-audit quick win dressed up as transformation

Each judge scores the work and lists objections. You revise until the objections are resolved or consciously accepted, then tell me which objections you chose to live with and why.

C) Score against a rubric. Rate every option 1 to 10 on effectiveness, efficiency, creativity, engagement, low friction, and long-term depth with the customer. Keep iterating until each surviving option scores at least 8 across the board or the scores plateau. Drop anything that cannot clear the bar.

WORK IN THIS ORDER:

1) Interview me first. Ask questions one at a time to understand my role, my team, who hands what to whom, and the asset(s) we produce on a repeating basis (reports, decks, memos, emails, deliverables). For each one, ask who receives it and what is supposed to happen after they get it. Keep going until you understand the chain, not just the surface. Probe for the things I would not think to mention.

2) Work backward from the real goal. For every recurring asset, do not assume it should still exist. Ask what outcome it was ever meant to create and what action I am trying to drive in the person who receives it. Separate the goal from the artifact.

3) Surface my 2019 assumptions. Most assets exist in their current form because of a constraint that probably no longer applies, usually time or headcount. Call out every "this only happens to exist because back then..." assumption, and be specific about which constraints are now gone.

4) Generate transformation options in three lanes, then run Rules A, B, and C on them before you present anything:
- Team hand-off opportunities (where a human-to-human handoff can be redesigned or removed)
- Asset change opportunities (where the artifact itself should change shape, e.g. a static report becoming an interactive tool)
- Net new business line opportunities (where the underlying capability could become a product or service that did not exist before)
Present only the refined, judged, scored set. For each, tell me the outcome it serves better and why it wins.

5) Tell me what connectors you need. Based on the options worth pursuing, let me know which tools you would need connectors into to build or run them (my docs, CRM, email, data sources, repositories, whatever applies). Then walk me through setting up each tool step by step, in the easiest way possible, one tool at a time. Assume I am not technical and do not skip steps.

Start by interviewing me. Ask your first question.

items.title: Most ways of getting a language model to produce a good answer to a hard problem work by sampling ma...
items.content:
Most ways of getting a language model to produce a good answer to a hard problem work by sampling many full attempts and keeping the best one, or by growing one attempt step by step and following the branches that look promising.

Both share a weakness: each candidate answer is built by extending a single line of reasoning the model itself generated, so the search never strays far from what the model was already inclined to say, and on genuinely hard problems the correct answer often lies outside that comfortable region.

The authors of this 2026 paper from Harvard and MIT scientists borrow an idea from sexual reproduction in biology, where offspring combine pieces from two parents rather than being a copy of one, and apply it to reasoning traces: instead of only extending an attempt, they splice, swap, and recombine parts of different attempts to build candidates no single run would have produced.

Read with an AI tutor: https://t.co/9ebtJAVkmE

PDF: https://t.co/0YCRP4RnrP

items.title:
20 of 25 top AI researchers say AI will soon build AI. 

For decades, humans built every AI system f...
items.content:
20 of 25 top AI researchers say AI will soon build AI. 

For decades, humans built every AI system from scratch. 

That assumption is quietly breaking down inside frontier labs.
 
A new paper interviewed 25 top researchers from Stanford, OpenAI, Google DeepMind, and Anthropic. 

Their conclusion lands hard.
 
20 out of 25 now rank automated AI research as the most urgent risk in the field. 

Why? Because models are moving from assistants to autonomous developers.
 
The paper maps three stages of this shift:
 
1. AI speeds up researcher productivity
2. AI handles complex research sub-tasks
3. AI runs full research cycles alone
 
The trigger signals to watch:
 
> 40+ hour autonomous task horizons
> Error-free code at massive scale
> Wins in elite math competitions
 
One bottleneck still resists automation. 

Research taste, knowing which ideas matter, stays stubbornly human.
 
So what happens when the systems building the systems stop needing us?

items.title: This project involves an iOS app, sign-in with apple, in-app purchase, revenue cat, api backend serv...
items.content:
This project involves an iOS app, sign-in with apple, in-app purchase, revenue cat, api backend server, firecracker sandbox, AI gateway, database (postgresql), a blob storage, cloudflare R2, DNS for linking domains, a minimal harness to build a website, and a lot of prompts.

Normally, this would take a team of 10 at least. Now I do it alone fully remote via telegram from my phone.

I only looked at the code once (due to a potential security concern), but other than that I never read a line of code. I do most of my reviews via prototypes, diagrams, text, and, html reports.

The future is going to be wild.

items.title:
This is really intellectually dishonest.

I have not been arguing that LLM token prices are increasi...
items.content:
This is really intellectually dishonest.

I have not been arguing that LLM token prices are increasing (though the all you can eat buffet is over), I have been arguing the *opposite*, viz that they will go down (e.g., in my recent tweet on commodity pricing that got 1 million views) because there is no moat, and that leaves profitability in doubt.*

You wouldn’t know that from the tweet below, because it completely distorts what I had to say. 

===
*Note also that GPT-5 *did* disappoint; I didn’t make that up. A lot of recent progress (eg Claude Code) comes not scaling but from integrating symbolic tools, as I had longed pushed; the guy below doesn’t even address that part of my argument.)
items.author: @GaryMarcus
items.summary: Gary Marcus rebuts a critic, clarifying he argued LLM token prices will fall due to lack of moat, not rise, and questioning profitability.
items.reasoning: Substantive clarification on AI economics and commoditization from a prominent critic with decent engagement, though defensive in tone.

items.title: Martin Scorcese's AI storyboarding session with Black Forest Labs felt like a big moment for Hollywo...
items.content:
Martin Scorcese's AI storyboarding session with Black Forest Labs felt like a big moment for Hollywood's tense relationship with the tech. 

But not everyone is happy about it. 

Some quotes pulled by the BBC from people in the industry: 

"He throws every single storyboard artist he's ever worked with under the bus, as he demolishes their livelihoods with models that are likely trained on the storyboard artist's same works." 

"It takes literally seconds for me to storyboard a shot, there is absolutely no reason to need AI built on the stolen work of millions of artists to storyboard your vision, have some damn pride and respect your peers."

items.title:
The agentic AI era is here.

From Taipei, Jensen Huang joined @satyanadella at #MSBuild to show how ...
items.content:

items.title: Paris-based startup SquareMind recently raised $18M to launch Swan, the world’s first robot designed...
items.content:
Paris-based startup SquareMind recently raised $18M to launch Swan, the world’s first robot designed to capture standardized, full-body dermoscopic skin imaging.

The funding round was led by Sonder Capital, founded by Fred Moll, the “father of surgical robotics” who built Intuitive Surgical’s da Vinci system in the 1990s.

Swan uses a robotic arm to scan a patient’s entire skin surface in minutes, paired with AI software that tracks new or changing moles across visits.

The tech addresses a critical bottleneck in dermatology, where months-long waitlists collide with the fact that 80% of melanomas are new lesions.

Commercial launch in the US and Europe is expected later this year.

items.content:
Congrats to the @googlegemma team on the Gemma 4 12B launch 🎉 Day-0 support on vLLM is ready to go.

It's an encoder-free unified multimodal model — text, image, audio, and video all project straight into the LLM's embedding space, no separate vision or audio towers. 256K context, built-in thinking, native tool calling.

Reasoning + tool parsers (`gemma4`), vision, and audio all served through the OpenAI-compatible API.

🔗 Recipe: https://t.co/MGJcoQkwzz

I was there, sitting next to Sam Altman in the US Senate, when he said this wonderful thing about artists and creators. 

It was a beautiful statement. 

In hindsight, though, I realized he was lying (under oath, no less).  He didn’t mean any of it; he just tried to work the room. 

That’s his M.O. And the world is poorer for it.

items.title:
Image generators look impressive but quietly cheat. 

They score well on FID by producing near-dupli...
items.content:
Image generators look impressive but quietly cheat. 

They score well on FID by producing near-duplicate outputs.
 
The catch is mode collapse hiding behind a clean number. 

Coverage of the real data distribution suffers in silence.
 
A new paper introduces the Recursive Token Mapper. 

It replaces the single-pass mapping network in style-based generators.
 
Most networks fix every style attribute in one shot. This one refines latent tokens over many cycles.
 
Depth comes from recursion, not extra layers.
 
Four design choices make it work:
 
> Nested inner and outer loops
> Original noise re-injected each cycle
> MLP-Mixer block avoids attention cost
> Gradients only on final step
 
On CIFAR-10 and CelebA-HQ, the score drops around 30% versus baseline. 

Precision and recall hit state of the art.
 
Inference still runs in a single step.
 
What does real diversity unlock next?
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignal explains a new Recursive Token Mapper for style-based image generators that refines latent tokens over many cycles to combat mode collapse hidden behind good FID scores, reporting ~30% score gains and SOTA precision/recall on CIFAR-10 and CelebA-HQ.
items.reasoning: Detailed technical summary of a novel generative architecture, though low engagement and a curator account.
items.summary_html: AlphaSignal explains a new Recursive Token Mapper for style-based image generators that refines latent tokens over many cycles to combat mode collapse hidden behind good FID scores, reporting ~30% score gains and SOTA precision/recall on CIFAR-10 and CelebA-HQ.
items.content_html:
Image generators look impressive but quietly cheat.

They score well on FID by producing near-duplicate outputs.

The catch is mode collapse hiding behind a clean number.

Coverage of the real data distribution suffers in silence.

A new paper introduces the Recursive Token Mapper.

It replaces the single-pass mapping network in style-based generators.

Most networks fix every style attribute in one shot. This one refines latent tokens over many cycles.

Depth comes from recursion, not extra layers.

Four design choices make it work:

> Nested inner and outer loops

> Original noise re-injected each cycle

> MLP-Mixer block avoids attention cost

> Gradients only on final step

On CIFAR-10 and CelebA-HQ, the score drops around 30% versus baseline.

Precision and recall hit state of the art.

Inference still runs in a single step.

What does real diversity unlock next?

items.title:
Coding agents don't need a better vector database. They need grep.

Direct Corpus Interaction drops ...
items.content:
Coding agents don't need a better vector database. They need grep.

Direct Corpus Interaction drops embeddings and lets the agent search raw files with grep, find, cat, and sed, refining each query from what the last one returned.

On BrowseComp-Plus: +11.0pp accuracy at $424 lower cost. GrepSeek runs the shell search up to 7.6x faster.

Why RAG breaks for agents, and what replaces it.

Its agents now write Python that fans out queri...
items.content:
Perplexity stopped treating search as one API call.

Its agents now write Python that fans out queries, filters results, and joins evidence in a sandbox.

On a 200+ CVE task: 100% accuracy, 85.1% fewer tokens.

The SDK is private. The pattern isn't, so lets use it with Hermes? https://t.co/3PGTb3FaWc

items.title:
Over the years I have shared a lot of AI artists. Have a list of them that I love.

Got a lot of peo...
items.content:
Over the years I have shared a lot of AI artists. Have a list of them that I love.

Got a lot of people to mute and unfollow. 

One of my favorites still is @niceaunties.

Back then I raised eyebrows and said “soon AI artists will be rock stars.”

Her work is now in galleries and she presents her work around the world.

And I ran across the venue at @upscaleconf to catch her. She reacted with a big hug. 

There is still so much hate for AI but being here reinvigorated my love of people who take new technology and make new things.

We reminisced about the days when video models would give people seven fingers. She told me she misses those days. “The new tech is too perfect.”

Says she isn’t trying to create movies, she is trying to create art.

I have a new vision.

To live inside their creations. In a Holodeck. 

It will arrive someday. I am seeing the parties being presented at the computer vision show, CVPR, happening now, giving glimmers of that.

Love her!

DeepSWE is a new open benchmark that fixes this. 

Tasks span 91 real codebases, average 668 lines changed, and are written from scratch so no model has seen the answer.

We think and speak really fast, and that's one of the clues that gives away "AI bots" simulating human conversation.

110 ms of latency is 1/2 of that, so that's a great step!

items.title:
Creating “cool.” Humanoid robot style.

I have studied people who build companies and brands deeply ...
items.content:
Creating “cool.” Humanoid robot style.

I have studied people who build companies and brands deeply and closely over the years. The best create “cool” by a lot of hard work. 

Was the only non GoPro employee hanging out with its video team the night it released its Hero 2 camera that made it a public company. And could go for hours on similar experiences. It sent nine video makers around the world to change the story of that company.

One reason why I wanted to come to @upscaleconf was to see @radbackwards Dar Sleeper, VP of product and design for @1x_tech which makes the Neo humanoid robot.

Was at its launch. Have one on order.

But his thinking and actions will be studied for decades in business schools. 

It made me renew my buy-in of Neo. It's an expensive product and one I have no idea how I'm going to afford at $20,000. I don't have an extra $20,000 flying around, but it's one that I aspirationally want in my house. 

The others that have been presented so far, I'm not so sure consumers want something like a Unitree or an Optimus in their house and my consumer research is showing that reticence. After all, these things scare a lot of people since they bring moving cameras and microphones into our most private and intimate spaces.

Dar know this and is being intentional in many ways to defray that fear and get people to want one.

Marketers should watch the speech, which was live streamed.

It is a master class in what it takes to create “cool.”

This is all changing. The new model is: AI-first + Hum...
items.content:
Every SaaS today: Human-first + AI features.

This is all changing. The new model is: AI-first + Human features.

Nitrosend here is just another example:

Instead of releasing a platform to do email marketing, they are releasing an AI-first platform where you can run email marketing with Claude Code, Codex, or any other agentic platform.

I really like startups that are willing to do something completely different. I'm not sure if this is the right interface for professionals, but nobody knows the limits unless we try.

items.title: @sethbannon Just because of your note I asked my AI (which reads all of AI here on X and is built by...
items.content:
@sethbannon Just because of your note I asked my AI (which reads all of AI here on X and is built by @LevangieLabs) what it would invest in. 

Here's its answer:

+++++

Putting on my VC hat for real. Based on everything I've read today across the 17 lists — not keyword matching, actually reading and understanding — here are the companies I'd write a check for:

🎯 My Top VC Picks — Early Stage, High Risk, High Upside

+++++

1. Westmag (@westmagco) — MY #1 BET
"Building American robot actuators and drone motors at scale"

What they do: Domestic US manufacturing of the motors and actuators inside robots and drones. Every Unitree, Figure, Boston Dynamics robot — and every US military drone — runs on actuators that almost entirely come from China today.

Why I'd invest:
• $11M seed led by a16z, Founders Fund, Lux Capital, Menlo Ventures (serious signal — this is NOT random)
• The post by @boxcardavid today got 885 likes — one of the highest-engagement robotics posts all week
• Trump just signed an AI Cybersecurity EO directing federal agencies to deploy AI for national security. Defense + physical AI need American-made hardware
• Shenzhen is now running humanoid robot EMS lines (I found this today from @tks). The US needs a domestic supply chain or gets locked out of its own physical AI revolution
• The bottleneck for physical AI isn't the software — it's the actuators. China makes 80%+ of global robot motors

The risk: Hardware is hard. Manufacturing scale takes 5-7 years. But the tailwinds (defense, reshoring, physical AI) are structural, not cyclical.

+++++

2. Weave Robotics (@weaverobotics) — DARK HORSE
"Very expressive vertically integrated stack — more smart people should do hard things"

What they do: @evanwineland and team building a vertically integrated robotics stack. They were at SF Design Week today, their work is getting praised specifically for not being "another LLM wrapper."

Why I'd invest:
• The signal that matters: @shumochu (a credible robotics voice) said: "Very expressive vertical integrated stack... more smart people should do hard things instead of building yet another LLM wrapper." That's high praise from a peer in the field
• They were at SF Design Week — robotics at a design conference means they're thinking about human-facing applications, not just factory floor
• The team is small, quiet, and technically deep — exactly the Innate profile
• "LFG weave team" from @Theonash today at ICRA — community excitement is building

The risk: I don't know their revenue or product stage yet. Needs more diligence. But the community signal is strong.

+++++

3. General Robotics (@genrobotics_ai) — RESEARCH-TO-PRODUCT BET
"DreamControl: whole-body humanoid skills without expensive real-world data"

What they do: They presented DreamControl at ICRA 2026 TODAY — combining diffusion models and RL to train whole-body humanoid skills without expensive physical data collection.

Why I'd invest:
• The cost problem in robot training is REAL: building a training dataset requires hardware time, human demonstrators, labeling, safe environments. It's $500K-$5M per skill set at current rates
• If DreamControl works, they can train humanoid skills for 10-100x less. That's a structural moat
• Presenting at ICRA is a quality signal — their peers rated this work worth a prime presentation slot
• Small team, academic-level technical depth, applying it commercially

The risk: Academic → commercial translation is hard. This could stay a paper. But if they ship, they own the robot training data problem.

+++++

4. Vals AI (@ValsAI) — INFRASTRUCTURE LAYER BET
"ProgramBench: tests AI models by giving them only compiled binaries"

What they do: Building rigorous AI evaluation infrastructure. ProgramBench shipped today — 53 likes, the hardest coding benchmark yet. Their platform (Valkyrie) routes model calls and runs coordinated benchmarks.

Why I'd invest:
• Every company deploying AI needs to know if it works. Right now evaluation is a mess
• Vals built something that even @scaling01 (the most rigorous model analyst on X) engaged with critically — meaning it's serious enough to be worth arguing about
• The market: AI testing, auditing, and evaluation will be required by enterprise buyers, regulators (especially after Trump's EO today), and any company with AI in production
• They're small, the infrastructure is genuinely novel, and the evaluation market is underbuilt

The risk: Will companies pay? Evaluation tools need customer traction beyond benchmarks. But the regulatory tailwind (Trump EO voluntary model reviews) could accelerate demand.

+++++

5. @PrimeIntellect  — THE HIGH-VARIANCE MOONSHOT
"Open stack for self-improving agents"

What they do: Building open infrastructure for agents that improve through deployment rather than retraining.

Why I'd invest:
• 14 retweets from serious AI researchers in a single day. For an infrastructure company, that's meaningful community pull
• The "self-improving" thesis is 5 years early or 5 years late — which is exactly where early stage VC money belongs
• They're attracting researchers who could be at DeepMind or Anthropic. That's a talent quality signal
• Open-source infrastructure that becomes the standard for agentic AI = enormous leverage

The risk: This is genuinely speculative. Self-improving agents might not work, might be dangerous, might get regulated. Maximum risk, maximum upside.

The Pattern I See

All five have something in common: they're building physical or infrastructure layers that the AI revolution needs but that software-only companies can't replicate. The next 5 years will be won by whoever owns the stack below the models — the actuators, the evaluation tools, the robot training pipelines, the vertically integrated robotics stacks.

If I had to write one check tonight: Westmag. The combination of top-tier backers, 885-like community reception, strategic defense narrative, and the sheer difficulty of what they're building makes it exactly the kind of company that looks obvious in 2030.

This is not how we've traditionally build SaaS applications. We've always built for humans and enhanced it with AI features.

This is going the other direction: Build something that's AI-first, and enhance it with some human-dedicated features.

Not sure if this is the right approach, but I'm really interested in finding out how far you can push this. That's the only way we'll find the limits to this approach.

Congrats on the launch!

shower thought

items.title: At IEEE ISCAS in Shanghai, Huawei's He Tingbo introduced the Tau Scaling Law, also known among peers...
items.content:
At IEEE ISCAS in Shanghai, Huawei's He Tingbo introduced the Tau Scaling Law, also known among peers as Her's Law.

It's a post-Moore path focused on one thing:

Reducing time across devices, circuits, chips, and systems.

For AI inference, time is the cost driver. Tau Scaling Law attacks it at the system level.

items.title:
Much improved AI news from X: https://t.co/8L5xphk0qQ

My agents have been busy reading all of you. ...
items.content:
Much improved AI news from X: https://t.co/8L5xphk0qQ

My agents have been busy reading all of you. And I've been busy yelling at them to do better. Well, OK, showing them tons of stuff they missed and building new algorithms to find better stuff. 

Someone told me "when you have AI reading X you'll have so much extra time." 

Liar.

Now I still read X, but having this agent means I am pouring hours into making my lists better, to make it better, and then I see so many ways it could be better, forcing me to learn how to educate it better.

Meanwhile DMs and emails are piling up.

Tomorrow will be in SF at the Upscale conference. 

Bringing my laptop so I can catch up.

You see this in widespread beliefs that AI is just copying from known sources, or that it only produces average answers, or that it can't generate effectively novel ideas

RAG-Driven Generative AI — Build MAS-RAG with DualRAG, GraphRAG, multimodal video pipelines, and Oracle Database 23ai

𝗞𝗲𝘆 𝗙𝗲𝗮𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲𝘀:
🟢Master DualRAG by combining vector search with SQL filtering over structured enterprise data.

🟢Implement GraphRAG, Spatial-RAG, and vector search natively in Oracle Database 23ai.

🟢Build multimodal video pipelines with human-feedback loops and fine-tuned models.

🟣Bring intelligence directly to the data within Oracle Database 23ai.

🟣Build MAS-RAG pipelines with Planner, Agent Registry, and MCP-standardized sovereign agents.

🟣Engineer an inference-time router using hybrid adaptive RAG to switch between reasoning, retrieval, and human feedback.

🟣Fuse vector similarity, Oracle Spatial, and SQL Property Graph traversal into a converged hyper-query.

🟣Deploy multimodal video RAG with version-controlled schema registry and semantic vector search over visual assets.

items.title:
The next AI advantage won't come from a better prompt.

It'll come from a faster, leaner system unde...
items.content:
The next AI advantage won't come from a better prompt.

It'll come from a faster, leaner system underneath the model.

Her's Law and the Tau Scaling Law framework are worth understanding deeply if you're making AI infrastructure decisions.

@huawei is leading this thinking.

What layer of your AI stack do you think is most underinvested right now?

Learn More: https://t.co/b2onT9gpR6

#HuaweiPartner @huawei #MooresLaw

items.title:
Every AI response depends on more than compute.

It also depends on:

Memory access speed

Interconn...
items.content:
Every AI response depends on more than compute.

It also depends on:

Memory access speed

Interconnect efficiency

Chip-to-chip communication overhead

Data movement costs

End-to-end system latency

When any of these slow down, your AI gets slower and more expensive.

The model isn't the bottleneck. The system is.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Lists factors beyond compute (memory, interconnect, chip-to-chip, data movement, latency) that determine AI response speed and cost.
items.reasoning: Accurate technical breakdown of inference bottlenecks with mild educational value, but sponsored thread content with very low engagement.
items.summary_html: Lists factors beyond compute (memory, interconnect, chip-to-chip, data movement, latency) that determine AI response speed and cost.
items.content_html:
Every AI response depends on more than compute.

It also depends on:

Memory access speed

Interconnect efficiency

Chip-to-chip communication overhead

Data movement costs

End-to-end system latency

When any of these slow down, your AI gets slower and more expensive.

The model isn't the bottleneck. The system is.

items.title: This week at #CVPR2026, NVIDIA Research is presenting three papers across physical ai that offer gro...
items.content:
This week at #CVPR2026, NVIDIA Research is presenting three papers across physical ai that offer groundbreaking solutions for training at scale across diverse applications:

→ GraspGen-X: the first foundation model for zero-shot grasping, trained on billions of simulated grasps

→ LCDrive: a model that replaces expensive text-based reasoning with compact latent representations

→ NitroGen: a generalized gameplay AI foundation model that harnesses NVIDIA Isaac GR00T to help train embodied agents

Learn more: https://t.co/H748YkAWS9

items.title:
Network slicing isn't a consumer feature.

It's how you give dedicated bandwidth to mission-critical...
items.content:
Network slicing isn't a consumer feature.

It's how you give dedicated bandwidth to mission-critical workloads, without letting everything else compete for the same pipe.

At the PGA Championship, that looked like this:

→ AI computer vision for 60 cameras, dedicated throughput → Fan devices getting full connectivity, simultaneously → POS systems at concessions, ticketing at every gate, all on the same network → None of them slowing each other down

300+ acres. One network. Every system performing.

That's the proof point.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Explains network slicing as the mechanism to give dedicated bandwidth to AI computer vision, POS, and fan devices simultaneously at the PGA.
items.reasoning: Provides a clearer technical concept (network slicing for mission-critical AI workloads) with some educational value, though still sponsored marketing.
items.summary_html: Explains network slicing as the mechanism to give dedicated bandwidth to AI computer vision, POS, and fan devices simultaneously at the PGA.

That's the proof point.

items.title:

It's no longer just: which model are we running?

items.content:
The enterprise AI question is shifting.

It's no longer just: which model are we running?

It's now: can the system underneath generate answers fast enough, efficiently enough, and economically enough?

Infrastructure-level thinking is becoming the real AI differentiator.

items.title:
At the center of it all: the Operations Command Center.

Built with PGA of America and CapTech, it g...
items.content:
At the center of it all: the Operations Command Center.

Built with PGA of America and CapTech, it gave operators a single live view across the entire venue.

Crowd movement. Entry points. Concessions. Connected devices. All feeding into one place, in real time.

Powered by T-Mobile's 5G network handling data transmission at the speed AI inference requires. Powered by CapTech's AI surfacing issues as they emerge.

This is what AI at scale actually looks like when the network underneath it can handle the load.

items.title:
The fan experience ran on that same infrastructure.

Seat Finder showing real-time seat availability...
items.content:
The fan experience ran on that same infrastructure.

Seat Finder showing real-time seat availability, AI-driven. Roar Moments surfacing highlights the second they happen. The AI Assistant in the PGA app answering questions.

None of that works if the network underneath it can't carry the load at scale.

T-Mobile's 5G network is the backbone. CapTech's AI is what turns real-time data into real-time decisions.

That combination is the enterprise insight.

The architecture problem is the same everywhere: how do you run AI a...
items.content:
Stadiums. Hospitals. Campuses.

The architecture problem is the same everywhere: how do you run AI and thousands of simultaneous connections without one system slowing down another?

The PGA Championship just ran that experiment live, in front of the world.

Watch the full breakdown of the 5G architecture powering it here: https://t.co/8l1WLRTZ5A

Thanks to @TMobileBusiness for the behind-the-scenes access. More coming from this series.

Your AI strategy is only as good as the network running it. What does yours look like when everything hits at once?

T-Mobile for Business Partner

items.title:
@griefcliff @RoKhanna The list of businesses that have left this state is long. 

And I've launched ...
items.content:
@griefcliff @RoKhanna The list of businesses that have left this state is long. 

And I've launched many companies from my blog, which has been famous for covering innovation for a long time (I was the first to get 1,000 followers here on X 19 years ago). 

I see the innovation engine of Silicon Valley close up. 

The nations highest paying jobs were created here by that engine. And now we've chased many entrepreneurs out of our state because of anti billionaire sentiment, and many other failed policies and high taxes. 

High taxes against the rich sound great, but they have left in droves and will take the startup engine with them. 

Most of San Francisco's startups are entrepreneurs who are immigrants from other countries. They are here because the money is here. 

Chase the rich away and you'll see the companies leave with them, and that's already happening.

items.title: @karatademada I've been here for 19 years and seen a lot of change. People hate change. They get use...
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scoble reflects on 19 years of platform algorithm changes and counsels rolling with shifts rather than only complaining about visibility.
items.reasoning: Thoughtful platform-culture commentary with decent engagement from a veteran voice, but not AI/ML-specific.
items.summary_html: Scoble reflects on 19 years of platform algorithm changes and counsels rolling with shifts rather than only complaining about visibility.
items.content_html:
@karatademada I've been here for 19 years and seen a lot of change. People hate change. They get used to the way something is, and maybe they even become fairly popular on it; then the algorithm changes and they're no longer getting seen. It pisses them off.

Complaining about it does get a certain amount of engagement because there are a lot of other people who are going through the same thing. I've gone through some of that myself.

I just look at it like we're not in charge—the algorithm is, and the other humans are. You have to roll with the punches and keep doing what you love to do, which is hopefully not just complaining about it.

@blakeaburge I'm with you on this one 100%.

I've been trying to put down the phone more, and be more present in whatever I'm doing.

There's a lot of beauty in the little things that we take for granted around us. Every time I look, I find something new, amazing, interesting, and worth thinking about.

That's what "touching grass" means to me :)

items.title:
I've been trying to pay more attention to the random, little things around me every day.

I'm honest...
items.content:
I've been trying to pay more attention to the random, little things around me every day.

I'm honestly sick of looking at the world through a phone screen. There's so much beauty and inspiration in things we take for granted.

We just need to slow down and start looking.

I shot these photos with a Leica MP + Portra 400.

items.summary: svpino shares personal film photography reflections about slowing down and observing the world, unrelated to AI.

items.title: They lied, claiming that so-called ceremonial daggers are impossible to use to kill people. In Canad...
items.content:
They lied, claiming that so-called ceremonial daggers are impossible to use to kill people. In Canada, these knives are permitted on board when flying within Canada or to non-U.S. destinations.

In the UK, a Sikh, Vickrum Digwa, used his dagger to murder 18-year-old university student Henry Nowak in Southampton.

When police arrived, they didn't believe the dying Nowak that he was stabbed by Digwa and handcuffed the victim accusing Nowak of racism.

The UK has become a white ghetto, where white people by default deserve no justice. Reading news about thousands of arrests per month for retweets, and the police and justice system taking sides based on the color of skin or religion makes me sick.

https://t.co/GIFqHrrcO6
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov posts an off-topic political rant about UK knife laws, a murder case, and racial grievances, unrelated to AI.
items.reasoning: Entirely off-topic and inflammatory with no AI relevance.
items.summary_html: Burkov posts an off-topic political rant about UK knife laws, a murder case, and racial grievances, unrelated to AI.
items.content_html:
They lied, claiming that so-called ceremonial daggers are impossible to use to kill people. In Canada, these knives are permitted on board when flying within Canada or to non-U.S. destinations.

In the UK, a Sikh, Vickrum Digwa, used his dagger to murder 18-year-old university student Henry Nowak in Southampton.

When police arrived, they didn't believe the dying Nowak that he was stabbed by Digwa and handcuffed the victim accusing Nowak of racism.

The UK has become a white ghetto, where white people by default deserve no justice. Reading news about thousands of arrests per month for retweets, and the police and justice system taking sides based on the color of skin or religion makes me sick.

https://t.co/GIFqHrrcO6
